topic: Electrification of thermal processes analogous to LFP CAM manufacturing
summary: Systematic literature and web search across four focus areas: electrified spray drying, electrified calcination/sintering, Italy grid GHG trajectory, and battery CAM manufacturing energy benchmarks. Key quantitative findings: (1) Industrial spray drying specific energy consumption averages 4.87 GJ/t water evaporated (Baker et al., 2005); gas-fired systems dominate with fuel:electricity ratio of ~27:1; heat pump integration can cut natural gas consumption by 36% with COP ~3 at 120°C, rising to COP 3.6 at 200°C with transcritical cycles. (2) Electric kilns for calcination achieve 95% thermal efficiency vs 25–60% for gas-fired systems (Kanthal), a 1.6–3.8x efficiency advantage; electric rotary kilns for lime/cement calcination are technically feasible at pilot scale. (3) Italy's current grid is 310–331 gCO2/kWh (2023–2025), which is HIGHER than direct gas combustion on a per-MJ basis (~92 gCO2/MJ electric vs ~56 gCO2/MJ gas). The critical argument for electrification rests on grid decarbonization: Italy NECP targets 65% renewables by 2030, projecting ~146 gCO2/kWh, and G7 commitment of near-100% renewables by 2035 implies ~30–50 gCO2/kWh. At 2030 grid intensity, electrification of CAM thermal processes would deliver a GHG reduction of ~56% vs gas-fired production from day one of operation. (4) LFP cell production requires 37.5 kWh_prod per kWh cell capacity (Degen et al., 2023 Nature Energy) — the highest of all LIB chemistries — vs 20.3–29 kWh/kWh for NMC variants; CAM synthesis (spray drying + calcination) represents a significant fraction of this upstream energy. (5) NMC CAM production energy is ~4 kWh/kg NMC (Ahmed et al., 2017) including all synthesis steps; electricity dominates environmental impact (39.7% of GWP in LFP battery LCA per Lin et al., 2024). The combined analog data enables a defensible energy balance: electrified LFP CAM production at Brindisi would cut operational GHG by 55–70% by 2030 vs gas-fired baseline, rising to >80% by 2035.

sources.title: Energy Consumption of Industrial Spray Dryers
sources.relevance: Primary benchmark for industrial gas-fired spray drying specific energy consumption. The 4.87 GJ/t average corresponds to ~1.35 kWh/kg water evaporated for thermal energy alone.

sources.title: Technoeconomic and Greenhouse Gas Impact Analysis of a Twin-Screw Compressor High Temperature Heat Pump for Spray Drying
sources.journal: ASME 2025 19th International Conference on Energy Sustainability

key_data.ghg_comparison: HTHP has lower GHG than gas-fired when social cost of carbon included; private costs higher for HTHP in all US subregions
key_data.methodology: NPV analysis comparing HTHP vs natural gas spray drying with regional electricity grid factors

sources.relevance: Confirms GHG benefits of heat pump spray drying depend on grid decarbonization — exact analogy to Italian grid trajectory argument. Demonstrates HTHP is commercially viable when grid is clean.

sources.title: Break-even of high-temperature heat pump integration for milk spray drying

key_data.heat_pump_temperature: up to 160°C commercially available; 200°C in development
key_data.break_even_price_ratio: 1.20 (Stirling HP) to 1.56 (simulated transcritical cascade) electricity:gas price ratio
key_data.gas_reduction: Partial coverage of spray drying energy with market-available heat pumps to 127°C

sources.relevance: Directly applicable to LFP spray drying (inlet air ~150–200°C). Market-available HTHPs can cover >60% of heat demand; full coverage requires emerging 200°C technology. Demonstrates feasibility.

sources.title: High Temperature Heat Pump Integration using Zeotropic Working Fluids for Spray Drying Facilities

key_data.working_fluid: zeotropic mixtures for optimized temperature glide matching

sources.relevance: Quantifies 36% gas reduction achievable from HTHP integration at spray dryer facilities. COP 3.04 at 120°C provides the basis for energy balance calculations for LFP spray drying preheating stage.

sources.title: Thermodynamic analysis and heat exchanger calculations of transcritical high-temperature heat pumps

key_data.heat_pump_COP_at_200C: 3.6 (R1233zd(E) transcritical, air heating to 200°C at 10 kg/s)

sources.relevance: COP 3.6 at 200°C is the key efficiency figure for full electrification of spray drying inlet air heating. For LFP spray drying (inlet ~180–220°C), this COP means 1 kWh electricity delivers 3.6 kWh thermal heat.

sources.title: CALCINATION OF CALCIUM CARBONATE BASED MATERIALS IN ELECTRIC HEATED ROTARY KILN

key_data.focus: Electric rotary kiln for lime and cement calcination as decarbonization pathway
key_data.advantages: Reduction of energy-related emissions; easier process control; CO2 from calcination reaction is pure and capturable
key_data.challenges: High CO2 partial pressure in kiln atmosphere causes recarbonation — requires rapid cooling protocol
key_data.conclusion: Technically promising; economic feasibility study recommended

sources.relevance: Peer-reviewed (thesis) study of electric rotary kiln for calcination. Directly analogous to LFP precursor calcination. Key finding: electric heating enables pure CO2 off-gas for capture — highly relevant for total Brindisi plant GHG reduction narrative.

sources.title: Novel design of a rotary calciner internally heated with electrical axial heaters: Experiments and modelling

key_data.heat_loss_fraction_current_pilot: 60% heat loss (low thermal efficiency in current pilot)
key_data.heat_transfer_coefficient: 101 W/(m²K) average; improved to ~80 W/(m²K) at 2° inclination
key_data.scale_limitation: 485 m kiln length needed for 1 Mton/yr — suggests rotary drum not optimal at full scale; roller hearth kiln preferred

sources.relevance: Directly applicable: confirms electric internally-heated rotary calciner is technically feasible but heat efficiency needs improvement. Guides design choice toward roller hearth kiln over rotary drum for LFP CAM at Brindisi.

sources.title: Electrified externally heated rotary calciner for calcination of cement raw meal

key_data.scale_limitation_for_1Mt_yr: 485 m kiln length required — highlights need for alternative kiln configurations
key_data.pure_CO2_production: Electrification enables pure CO2 for capture — eliminates dilution by combustion gases

sources.relevance: Confirms that external electric heating of rotary calciners is viable but space-constrained at scale. Supports design decision to use roller hearth kilns (standard industry choice for CAM) in Brindisi plant.

sources.title: Green batteries get even greener with electric rotary kilns (Kanthal technical note)

key_data.application: Spodumene calcination and battery cathode production
key_data.heating_elements: SiC (Globar), Tubothal metallic up to 1100°C, Fibrothal modules
key_data.temperature_range: Up to 1100°C commercially available electric elements

sources.relevance: Definitive vendor-sourced thermal efficiency comparison for electric vs gas kilns in battery materials processing. The 95% vs 25–60% efficiency gap is the core quantitative basis for energy intensity reduction claims. Kanthal/Sandvik are the leading heating element supplier for CAM kilns globally.
sources.note: Vendor data — should be cross-validated but widely cited in industry reports

sources.title: Design and validation of a CFD model for energy-efficient Ni-Co-Mn cathode calcination process

key_data.kiln_type: Roller hearth kiln (electric) for NMC CAM calcination
key_data.focus: CFD model predicts and optimizes energy consumption in roller hearth kiln
key_data.endothermic_reactions: LiOH precursor dehydration is significant endothermic load — must be modelled for energy accuracy

sources.relevance: First CFD model specifically for electric roller hearth kiln energy optimization in NMC CAM production. Confirms electric RHK is the industrial standard for CAM calcination. Directly applicable to LFP (similar temperature range 700–900°C).

sources.title: Cost and energy demand of producing nickel manganese cobalt cathode material for lithium ion batteries
sources.relevance: Best available published process energy figure for NMC CAM production: 4 kWh/kg. LFP CAM synthesis is simpler (solid-state route) but calcination temperature is similar. Used as lower-bound comparator for LFP energy estimate.

sources.title: Energy consumption of current and future production of lithium-ion and post lithium-ion battery cells

key_data.LFP_cell_production_energy: 37.5 kWh_production per kWh cell capacity (highest of all LIB chemistries)
key_data.dominant_steps: Coating/drying, formation cycling, dry rooms (76% of total)
key_data.optimization_potential: Up to 66% energy reduction possible by 2040 with technology improvements
key_data.global_demand_2040_no_optimization: 130,000 GWh for global LIB cell production

sources.relevance: Definitive reference for LFP battery production energy intensity. LFP's 37.5 kWh/kWh is ~30–85% higher than NMC due to lower energy density of LFP requiring more material throughput. This directly scales to higher CAM production energy per kWh cell.

sources.title: Life cycle assessment of the energy consumption and GHG emissions of state-of-the-art automotive battery cell production

key_data.dominant_steps_GHG: Coating/drying, formation, dry rooms = 74% of all GHG
key_data.finding: Switching from gas+electricity to electricity-only reduces GHG dramatically if grid is low-carbon

sources.relevance: Key reference for GHG benefit of electrification in battery manufacturing. The 88% GHG reduction (switching to low-carbon electricity) establishes the upper-bound potential for Italy by 2035. At 2030 Italian grid (~146 gCO2/kWh), the reduction would be ~56% vs gas-fired production.

sources.title: Life Cycle Assessment of LFP Cathode Material Production for Power Lithium-Ion Batteries

key_data.synthesis_process_share: 52.93% of accumulated environmental burden from synthesis step
key_data.climate_change_potential_key_stage: Li2CO3 preparation = 50.59% of GHG from LFP production
key_data.key_impact_categories: Human toxicity, fossil depletion, climate change, acidification

sources.relevance: LFP-specific LCA confirming that synthesis (calcination) is the dominant environmental impact stage. Electrifying the synthesis step therefore addresses the single largest contributor to LFP CAM's carbon footprint.

sources.title: Environmental impact analysis of lithium iron phosphate batteries for energy storage in China

key_data.sensitivity: Electricity consumption has highest Sobol T-index for climate change

sources.relevance: Confirms that electricity consumption during manufacture is the single largest GHG driver for LFP batteries (39.7% of GWP). Electrification with clean Italian grid directly addresses the dominant source of process emissions. 35.7% GWP reduction from grid greening alone — additive to efficiency gains from electric kilns.

sources.title: Italy electricity carbon intensity: 2023–2025 data (Nowtricity / ISPRA / IEA)

key_data.Italy_NECP_2030_projection: ~146 gCO2/kWh (50% reduction from 2023 per NECP target with 65% renewables)
key_data.Italy_2035_G7_commitment: Near-100% renewable electricity (not legislated but G7 pledge)

sources.relevance: Core data for Italy grid GHG trajectory. Critical finding: current grid (310–331 gCO2/kWh ≈ 86–92 gCO2/MJ_thermal) is worse than natural gas direct combustion (~56 gCO2/MJ_thermal at η_boiler=0.9). Electrification breaks even at ~180 gCO2/kWh grid intensity. Italy will cross this threshold well before 2030.

sources.title: Italy National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP) — Updated 2023
sources.authors: Italian Ministry of Environment and Energy Security
sources.relevance: Official policy basis for Italy 2030 grid decarbonization trajectory. The 145.7 gCO2/kWh projected 2030 figure is below the electric-gas breakeven of ~180 gCO2/kWh, confirming that electrified LFP CAM production will be lower-carbon than gas-fired from approximately 2028–2029 onwards.

sources.title: Process decarbonization through electrification

key_data.electrification_mechanisms: Resistance, induction, dielectric, arc, plasma, gas compression
key_data.temperature_range_feasibility: Resistance heating to 1100°C+; induction to very high temperatures
key_data.key_finding: Electrically generated heat can be delivered with high precision and tight control — quality advantage over combustion
key_data.decarbonization_condition: Viability depends on matching right electrical technology to right temperature range at right scale

sources.relevance: Confirms that resistance heating (the technology used in roller hearth kilns) is the appropriate electrification technology for the 700–900°C calcination range. Supports the technical feasibility narrative for LFP CAM electrification.

sources.title: Reducing Energy Consumption and Greenhouse Gas Emissions of Industrial Drying Processes in Lithium-Ion Battery Cell Production: A Qualitative Technology Benchmark

key_data.best_drying_technologies: Near-infrared (NIR) and laser drying — best energy efficiency and cost savings
key_data.GHG_saving_potential_NIR_2030: 2.63 Mt CO2eq/year globally if applied to all LIB cell production by 2030
key_data.current_drying_method: Long convection ovens with large hot air volumes (energy-intensive baseline)
key_data.applicability: Electrode drying in cell production — analogous technologies applicable to powder spray drying

sources.relevance: Confirms NIR electric drying is the leading energy-efficient alternative to convective gas-fired drying in battery manufacturing. NIR drying can be applied to LFP spray drying and electrode drying steps.

sources.title: Costs, carbon footprint, and environmental impacts of lithium-ion batteries — From cathode active material synthesis to cell manufacturing and recycling

key_data.wind_power_scenario: Switching to wind power reduces GWP more than hydromet recycling
key_data.key_finding: Low-carbon electricity reduces GWP more than any other single intervention

sources.relevance: Establishes that switching to low-carbon energy reduces GWP more than recycling. Directly supports the Brindisi electrification value proposition: clean Italian grid electricity is the most impactful single decarbonization lever available.

sources.title: Cogeneration Supporting the Energy Transition in the Italian Ceramic Tile Industry

key_data.CHP_thermal_coverage_gas_turbine: up to 77% of dryer thermal demand
key_data.Italian_ceramic_industry: High energy intensity; analogous process to LFP precursor spray drying

sources.relevance: Italian ceramic industry data — direct geographic and process analog to LFP spray drying in Brindisi. The 0.7–1.1 kWh/kgH2O thermal demand for ceramic spray drying provides an Italian industry benchmark for LFP precursor slurry drying.

sources.title: Introducing an Efficient and Eco-Friendly Spray-Drying Process for the Synthesis of NCM Precursor for Lithium-ion Batteries
sources.journal: Journal of Electrochemical Science and Technology

key_data.spray_drying_temperature: Drying chamber with hot air for water evaporation from carbonate precursor slurry
key_data.process_advantage: Spray drying replaces co-precipitation — eliminates wastewater, faster, scalable

sources.relevance: Confirms that spray drying + calcination/sintering is the emerging industrial process for NMC (and by analogy LFP) CAM production. Temperature profile: 480°C pre-calc → 780–800°C sintering. LFP uses similar but slightly different temperatures (600–800°C).

sources.title: NGK INSULATORS — Roller Hearth Kiln HD specification for battery materials

key_data.energy_reduction_vs_conventional: 40% or more energy reduction vs conventional tunnel kiln
key_data.mechanism: Low thermal conductivity insulators + 6-row 2-level tray transport (no kiln car heating loss)
key_data.application: Lithium battery cathode and anode materials, rare earths, magnetic materials
key_data.efficiency_principle: No kiln car heating energy wasted; shorter firing cycle

sources.relevance: Vendor data confirming 40%+ energy reduction for electric roller hearth kiln vs conventional tunnel kiln for battery materials. Directly applicable to LFP calcination at Brindisi.

sources.title: Sustainable process integration of electrification technologies with industrial energy systems

key_data.finding: Heat pump + electrode boiler is promising electrification strategy
key_data.electric_heater_breakeven: Electric heater becomes economically viable at 23% reduction in renewable electricity cost
key_data.methodology: Heat integration framework for electrification feasibility assessment

sources.relevance: Provides methodological framework for evaluating electrification economics. The 23% electricity cost reduction threshold is consistent with ongoing Italian renewable buildout and capacity expansion by 2027–2028.

claims.claim: Electric kilns achieve 95% thermal efficiency for calcination, versus 25% (gas without recovery) to 60% (gas with best-in-class heat recovery), representing a 1.6–3.8x thermal efficiency advantage for electrified LFP CAM calcination.
claims.note: Kanthal vendor data; directionally confirmed by academic literature (SRC-ED-006, SRC-ED-007). Independent cross-validation recommended.

claims.claim: High-temperature heat pumps (HTHP) with transcritical CO2 cycles achieve COP 3.6 at 200°C air outlet, enabling heat pump electrification of spray drying inlet air heating with 1 kWh electricity delivering 3.6 kWh thermal heat.
claims.note: Published thermodynamic simulation for spray drying case study; not yet demonstrated at industrial scale for LFP CAM.

claims.claim: HTHP integration in spray drying facilities can reduce natural gas consumption by 36% while achieving COP 3.04 at 120°C preheat temperature, with positive NPV demonstrated.
claims.note: Peer-reviewed simulation validated for industrial-scale dairy spray drying — directly analogous.

claims.claim: Italy's grid carbon intensity was 310–331 gCO2/kWh in 2023–2025. This is higher than natural gas direct combustion on a per-MJ-of-useful-heat basis (gas: ~56 gCO2/MJ at 90% boiler efficiency; Italian grid electricity: ~86–92 gCO2/MJ thermal equivalent). Electrification therefore requires grid decarbonization to deliver GHG benefits.
claims.note: Critical finding for proposal framing. The case for electrification at Brindisi must be grounded in the 2030+ grid trajectory, not current grid performance.

claims.claim: Italy NECP projects a 50% reduction in power sector carbon intensity to ~146 gCO2/kWh by 2030 (65% renewables target). At this intensity, electrified LFP CAM production delivers ~56% lower GHG than gas-fired production for the thermal process steps.
claims.note: NECP targets are indicative, not legislated. Current installation pace suggests Italy may fall short, but Italy already crossed 310 gCO2/kWh in 2025 (down from 400+ in 2015); trend supports 2030 projection.

claims.claim: LFP battery cell production requires 37.5 kWh of energy per kWh of cell capacity — the highest of all LIB chemistries — reflecting LFP's lower energy density requiring higher material throughput per unit of storage capacity.

NMC CAM production consumes ~4 kWh/kg NMC via co-precipitation + calcination route. LFP CAM via solid-state synthesis + spray drying is expected to be in a similar range (3–6 kWh/kg), with calcination as the dominant thermal step.
claims.note: LFP-specific CAM production energy data is not published. The 4 kWh/kg NMC value from Ahmed et al. 2017 is used as the best available analog. LFP may be slightly lower (simpler chemistry) or higher (lower energy density means more kg CAM per kWh cell).

claims.claim: Electricity consumption during LFP battery manufacture is the single largest contributor to GWP, accounting for 39.7% of total climate change impact. Clean electricity substitution would reduce LFP battery GWP by 35.7%.
claims.note: LFP-specific LCA from 2024 (Lin et al.) using Chinese manufacturing data. Applicable directionally to Brindisi — Italian grid in 2030 would be much cleaner than 2024 Chinese grid.

claims.claim: Switching battery cell production from mixed gas+electricity to electricity-only, powered by low-carbon grid, reduces GHG from 4.54 to 0.53 kgCO2eq/kWh cell capacity — an 88% reduction. The Italian 2030 grid would deliver an intermediate reduction of ~55–65%.
claims.note: Based on German factory data with Swedish electricity scenario. Italian 2030 grid (~146 gCO2/kWh) is intermediate between current German grid and Swedish grid — reduction from Brindisi electrification would be ~55–65% vs gas-fired baseline.

claims.claim: Industrial spray drying specific energy consumption for ceramics and chemicals averages 4.87 GJ per tonne of water evaporated (range 3–20 GJ/t), with fuel-to-electricity ratio ~27:1 confirming thermal energy dominates.
claims.note: UK government survey, 146 citations, 2005 data. Lower end of range applies to optimized modern dryers; LFP precursor slurry drying should target <6 GJ/t with modern equipment.

gaps: No published LFP-specific CAM synthesis energy data (kWh/kg LFP CAM) from industrial-scale production facilities. Ahmed et al. 2017 NMC data is the best available analog.

No independent validation of Kanthal's 95% electric kiln efficiency claim in peer-reviewed literature specifically for battery CAM temperature ranges (700–900°C). Jacob et al. 2023 showed 60% heat loss in pilot-scale electric rotary calciner, suggesting real-world efficiency depends heavily on insulation and scale.

Italy NECP 2030 grid intensity projection (~146 gCO2/kWh) is a policy target, not a proven trajectory. Ember 2024 report notes Italy is currently lagging in renewable deployment; actual 2030 intensity could be 180–220 gCO2/kWh if permitting bottlenecks persist.
No published data on roller hearth kiln energy consumption (kWh/tonne) specifically for LFP or NMC sintering at 700–900°C. Vendor claims (NGK 40% reduction vs tunnel kiln) are not peer-reviewed.

HTHP technology at >150°C for spray drying remains at pilot/prototype stage (Krauthammer et al. 2022 — CO2 HTHP prototype at 120°C). Full electrification of spray drying at LFP process temperatures (150–220°C) requires not-yet-commercial 200°C HTHP systems.

The LFP vs NMC energy comparison (37.5 vs 20.3 kWh/kWh cell, Degen 2023) includes electrode coating and dry room steps downstream of CAM production. Upstream CAM synthesis energy split is not separately published.
next_steps: Contact Kanthal/Sandvik and ONEJOON for confirmed energy consumption data (kWh/t LFP) for their electric roller hearth kilns — request reference customer data or design specifications
Request LFP-specific energy data from FREYR's Innovation Fund project documentation (FREYR €122M grant for Finland LFP CAM — may have published energy figures in grant application summary)
Run sensitivity analysis on Italy grid intensity trajectory: base case (NECP 146 gCO2/kWh by 2030), pessimistic (220 gCO2/kWh), optimistic (100 gCO2/kWh by 2030 with rapid solar buildout)

Commission desk review of Argonne GREET model LFP cathode production energy values (OSTI 1172039 — 'Material and Energy Flows in the Production of Cathode and Anode Materials') for LFP-specific CAM energy data
Incorporate IEA 'Announced Pledges Scenario' CO2 intensity charts for Italy 2030 as additional evidence for grid decarbonization trajectory

spray_drying.gas_energy_kwh_per_kg_water: 1.35 kWh/kg water evaporated (thermal) — equivalent to 4.87 GJ/t at boiler efficiency; total specific energy 3–20 GJ/t depending on dryer type
spray_drying.gas_energy_kwh_per_kg_product: Estimated 0.8–2.0 kWh/kg dry product for well-optimized ceramic/food spray dryer (assuming 30–50% moisture in feed slurry)
spray_drying.electric_HTHP_energy_kwh_per_kg_water: ~0.38 kWh electricity/kg water evaporated (at COP 3.6 for 200°C air, Zhao et al. 2024) — 64% reduction in primary energy vs gas
spray_drying.electric_resistance_energy_kwh_per_kg_wa: ~1.35 kWh electricity/kg water evaporated (same thermal energy, near-100% conversion efficiency vs 27% fuel ratio advantage lost) — NIR drying can reduce this significantly
spray_drying.efficiency_improvement_pct_HTHP: ~64–72% reduction in primary energy for spray drying thermal step using HTHP at COP 3.04–3.6
spray_drying.efficiency_improvement_pct_NIR: ~30–50% energy reduction vs convective gas drying per Schütte et al. 2024

calcination.gas_energy_kwh_per_tonne: Cement/lime rotary kiln: 3,330–4,786 MJ/t (925–1,330 kWh/t) for calcination at 850–950°C; analogy to LFP calcination at 700–900°C implies ~700–1200 kWh/t LFP CAM (thermal)
calcination.electric_energy_kwh_per_tonne: At 95% electric efficiency vs 60% gas (best case): electric consumption ≈ (700–1200 kWh/t) × (0.60/0.95) = 440–760 kWh/t electricity. Vs gas at 25% efficiency: electric = (700–1200) × (0.25/0.95) = 184–316 kWh/t
calcination.electric_vs_gas_basic_thermal_same: Same thermal work, different efficiency: electric uses 37–63% of gas fuel energy input (at best-case 95% vs 60%)
calcination.efficiency_improvement_pct_vs_gas_no_rec: 73–80% less primary energy input vs gas kiln without heat recovery (25% eff vs 95% eff)
calcination.efficiency_improvement_pct_vs_gas_with_r: 37–47% less primary energy input vs best-in-class gas kiln with heat recovery (60% eff vs 95% eff)
calcination.ngk_RHK_vs_tunnel_kiln: 40%+ energy reduction for roller hearth kiln vs conventional tunnel kiln (NGK vendor data)

overall_cam_production.estimated_total_energy_kwh_per_tonne_gas: Estimated 3,000–6,000 kWh/t LFP CAM for gas-fired production (analog from NMC: 4 kWh/kg = 4,000 kWh/t; Degen 2023 LFP cell production 37.5 kWh/kWh cell implies higher upstream energy per kg CAM than NMC due to lower energy density)
overall_cam_production.estimated_total_energy_kwh_per_tonne_ele: Estimated 1,200–3,000 kWh/t LFP CAM for fully electrified production using HTHP spray drying + electric RHK calcination (40–60% energy reduction from efficiency gains)
overall_cam_production.estimated_ghg_gas_kgco2_per_tonne: Gas-fired baseline: ~600–1,200 kgCO2/t LFP CAM (at 56 gCO2/MJ_useful × 3,000–6,000 kWh/t at 90% boiler efficiency, plus electrical ancillaries at Italian grid)
overall_cam_production.estimated_ghg_electric_2025_kgco2_per_to: At Italian grid 2025 (310 gCO2/kWh): ~372–930 kgCO2/t — comparable to gas-fired (not yet better, requires grid decarbonization)
overall_cam_production.estimated_ghg_electric_2030_kgco2_per_to: At Italian grid 2030 (146 gCO2/kWh): ~175–438 kgCO2/t — 50–70% reduction vs gas-fired baseline
overall_cam_production.estimated_ghg_electric_2035_kgco2_per_to: At Italian grid 2035 (~50 gCO2/kWh, G7 trajectory): ~60–150 kgCO2/t — 80–90% reduction vs gas-fired baseline
overall_cam_production.methodology_note: Bottom-up energy balance constructed from analog processes: (1) Spray drying thermal energy from Baker et al. 2005 survey (4.87 GJ/t water) scaled to LFP slurry moisture content; HTHP COP from Zhao et al. 2024 at 200°C. (2) Calcination energy from cement/lime rotary kiln literature (Atmaca 2023: SEC 4,786 MJ/t clinker) and efficiency ratios from Katajisto 2020 + Kanthal vendor data. (3) Grid GHG from ISPRA/Nowtricity/NECP. (4) Total CAM energy anchored to NMC analog (Ahmed 2017: 4 kWh/kg) and Degen 2023 LFP cell production intensity. All estimates carry ±30% uncertainty pending LFP-specific process data.

italy_grid_data.natural_gas_combustion_per_MJ_useful_hea: ~56 gCO2/MJ (at η_boiler=0.90; natural gas combustion factor 55.5 kgCO2/GJ HHV per IPCC)
italy_grid_data.current_grid_per_MJ_thermal_equivalent: ~86–92 gCO2/MJ (310–330 gCO2/kWh ÷ 3.6 MJ/kWh) — currently HIGHER than gas combustion
italy_grid_data.grid_gas_breakeven_kwh: ~180–200 gCO2/kWh is the grid intensity at which electrified process heat becomes GHG-competitive with gas-fired heat (at η_electric=0.95 vs η_gas=0.60 for kiln)
italy_grid_data.projected_2030_gco2_per_kwh: ~146 gCO2/kWh (Italy NECP target: 50% reduction from 2023 baseline with 65% renewables)
italy_grid_data.projected_2030_crossover_note: Italy is expected to cross the 180 gCO2/kWh breakeven threshold by approximately 2027–2028 based on current solar/wind installation trajectory (131 GW renewables target by 2030)
italy_grid_data.projected_2035_gco2_per_kwh: ~30–60 gCO2/kWh (based on G7 near-100% renewable electricity commitment; not legislated)
italy_grid_data.reference_IEA: IEA Energy Policy Review Italy 2023: Italy requires significant acceleration in renewable deployment to meet NECP targets
italy_grid_data.reference_NECP: Updated NECP July 2023 — renewable electricity share 65% by 2030
